entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Go-Live Success: A Comprehensive Checklist for Driving User Adoption
entry.content:
Implementing SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) successfully requires more than just technical deployment—it demands a strategic approach to ensure optimal performance, user adoption, and long-term value. This comprehensive guide outlines the critical areas organizations must address to achieve a successful SAC go-live for both Analytics and Planning scenarios.

The Foundation: Four Pillars of SAC Success

A successful SAC implementation rests on four fundamental pillars that must be carefully evaluated and optimized:

  1. Data Acquisition and Live Connection
  2. Modeling Excellence
  3. Story Design Optimization
  4. Ongoing House Keeping

Let's explore each pillar in detail to understand how they contribute to driving adoption and consumption in your SAC environment.

Pillar 1: Data Acquisition and Live Connection

The foundation of any analytics solution lies in robust data connectivity. For SAC implementations, this means ensuring your infrastructure can handle the demands of real-time analytics and planning scenarios.

Network and Infrastructure Considerations

Your network configuration becomes critical, especially when implementing live connections to source systems. This includes:

  • Device compatibility testing across laptops and mobile devices to ensure consistent user experience
  • Source system capacity evaluation for live scenarios to prevent performance bottlenecks
  • Tenant capacity planning, particularly crucial for import scenarios and planning workloads

End-to-End Connection Validation

A comprehensive review of your SAC connections should cover multiple technical layers:

  • Cloud Connector configuration and optimization
  • Authentication and Single Sign-On (SSO) setup
  • User authorization frameworks
  • Browser behavior testing in realistic business scenarios

These elements work together to create a seamless user experience that encourages adoption rather than creating barriers.

Pillar 2: Modeling Excellence

Effective modeling in SAC requires balancing functionality with performance, ensuring your solution serves actual business needs rather than simply showcasing available data.

Requirement Alignment and Simplification

Before diving into complex modeling scenarios, evaluate whether your requirements align with SAC's standard capabilities. Ask yourself:

  • Do your needs fit SAC's out-of-the-box functionality?
  • Are you creating unnecessary workarounds for complex workflows?
  • Is your commenting and large table handling approach sustainable?

Business-Focused Design Philosophy

The most successful SAC implementations follow a principle of designing for the business goal rather than building from all available data. This approach involves:

  • Keeping models simple and efficient by using only truly necessary data
  • Reducing data volume early in the modeling process
  • Avoiding unnecessary complexity that can hinder maintenance and scaling
  • Designing for maintainability to ensure long-term success

SAC Best Practices Implementation

Following established SAC best practices ensures your solution is secure, reliable, and provides smooth workflows. Key areas include:

  • Proper date handling and hierarchy design
  • Robust access control implementation
  • Comprehensive validation frameworks
  • Optimized planning area configuration

Pillar 3: Story Design Optimization

User experience in SAC stories directly impacts adoption rates. Poor performance or clunky interfaces can quickly derail even the most well-intentioned analytics initiatives.

Script and Event Optimization

Pay special attention to story pages utilizing scripts in critical events:

  • Review "onInitialization" and "onActive" event implementations
  • Eliminate unnecessary widget refreshes that can slow down user interactions
  • Optimize long-running data actions and multi-actions for better performance

Performance Testing and Validation

Comprehensive performance testing should cover both individual and concurrent usage scenarios:

  • Single-user performance optimization to ensure responsive individual experiences
  • High user concurrency testing to validate system behavior under realistic load conditions
  • Realistic scenario and volume testing to identify potential bottlenecks before go-live

Pillar 4: House Keeping and Ongoing Maintenance

Successful SAC implementations require ongoing attention to system health and optimization opportunities.

System Monitoring and Health Checks

Regular monitoring of your SAC environment should include:

  • Homepage System Overview reviews to identify potential issues early
  • Import job failure monitoring to ensure data freshness and reliability
  • Data action performance tracking to identify optimization opportunities
  • Long-running query analysis to prevent user experience degradation

Job Optimization and Scheduling

Efficient job management contributes significantly to overall system performance:

  • Review calendar job cadence regularly
  • Adjust or reduce job frequency where possible without impacting business requirements
  • Balance system resource utilization across different time periods

Driving Adoption Through Excellence

The ultimate goal of following this comprehensive checklist is to create an SAC environment that users want to engage with regularly. When data connections are reliable, models are intuitive, stories perform well, and the system runs smoothly, user adoption naturally follows.

Organizations that systematically address each of these four pillars typically see higher user engagement, better business outcomes, and stronger return on their SAC investment. The key is treating go-live not as a destination, but as the beginning of an ongoing optimization journey.

Next Steps for Your SAC Implementation

As you prepare for your SAC go-live or optimize an existing implementation, consider conducting a comprehensive review across all four pillars. Start with the areas that most directly impact your users' daily experience, then work systematically through the technical infrastructure elements that support those experiences. If you seek expert services and guidance for any of the areas please check our engagement plans and service portfolio.

Remember, successful SAC adoption is built on the foundation of technical excellence, user-centric design, and ongoing operational discipline. By following this checklist and maintaining focus on these core principles, your organization can maximize the value of its SAC investment while driving meaningful business outcomes through enhanced analytics and planning capabilities.

entry.title: Blog Series: Agentic Reporting - The Next Evolution of SAP Reporting
entry.content:
Most enterprises still operate in a world of dashboards that simply report what happened. KPIs move, charts update, and alerts fire when thresholds are crossed — yet none of this tells us whether the deviation is expected, normal for this period, or a real operational risk.

With AI, reporting can evolve from passive visualization to active, context‑aware decisioning.
Instead of waiting for users to interpret numbers, the system itself can observe, understand context, reason over patterns, decide significance, and take action.

This is Agentic Reporting — where insights behave like a junior analyst embedded inside your SAP landscape (S/4HANA, SAC, Datasphere, BTP), continuously interpreting changes and surfacing only what truly needs attention.

What Makes Reporting Agentic? 

Traditional reporting is static: 

  • Predefined KPIs 
  • Rigid thresholds 
  • IF → THEN rules 
  • No learning  

Agentic reporting is dynamic: 

  •  Observes data continuously 
  • Considers context — seasonality, patterns, user role, business calendar 
  •  Determines if a deviation is meaningful 
  • Recommends or triggers action 
  • Learns from outcomes  

Why Agents > Classic Alerts/Rigid Triggers 

Reporting Triggers: 

  • Static 
  • One KPI - One Action
  • Same action every time 
  • Push Only
  • No Context, No learning  

AI Agents: 

  • Adaptive, context-aware thresholds 
  • Understands correlations across KPIs 
  • Chooses the optimal action 
  • Learns over time 
  • Proactive + conversational 

 

Agentic Reporting Use Case: KPI Breach Monitoring

Use Case: KPI Breach Monitoring Tool

Traditional Approach: 

  • “If DSO > 60 days → Send email.” 
  • No context → many false alarms. 

Agentic Approach: 

  • “Is this deviation abnormal given today’s reality?” 

 Scenario A – 8% DSO Increase (No Alert) 

Agent checks context

  • It’s the end of quarter (seasonal spike expected)
  • A major customer’s payment cycle always delays during this period
  • The same pattern occurred in the last 3 quarters

Conclusion: The deviation is expected and harmless → No alert raised.

 Scenario B – Same 8% Increase Next Month (Alert!) 

Agent checks context

  • No end of quarter ❌
  • No seasonal explanation ❌
  • pattern does not match past 3 quarters ❌
  • Impacts cashflow ❌
  • Unusual vs history ❌

Conclusion: The deviation is unexpected and risky → Escalate with analysis

 Agent provides: 

  • Root-cause patterns ✔️
  • Customer contribution ✔️
  • Impact on forecast ✔️
  • Recommended actions ✔️

 The Bottom Line 

Agentic Reporting takes organizations from: 

Dashboards you look at → Dashboards that look out for you. 

 As SAP deepens Joule, AI, and agentic capabilities, this shift becomes not only possible — but necessary.

Next in the series:

  • Blog #2: Two Ways to Build Agentic Reporting in SAP: Python Agent on SAP BTP vs. Joule Studio Agent
  • Blog #3: How to Build a Python Agent for Agentic Reporting (Datasphere + BTP)
  • Blog #4: How to Build an SAP-native Joule Agent for Agentic Reporting

entry.title: はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - 計算の追加
entry.content:
本ブログシリーズはSAP Analytics CloudのBI機能を使ってダッシュボードやレポートの作成を行う方を対象とし、SAP Analytics CloudのBIにおける基本の操作やTipsをハンズオン形式でご紹介します。

本ブログでは、営業利益率や累計売上高など元々データとして入っていなかった値を計算させて表示される方法についてご紹介します。

 


 目次：

  • SAP Analytics Cloudにおける計算の追加
  • ストーリーでの計算式追加（営業利益率、累計売上高の計算）［ハンズオン］

※ ベースとなるサンプルストーリーの作成手順は、はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成 - SAP Communityでご紹介しています。

※ SAP Analytics Cloudは、四半期に１度のペースで製品のバージョンアップが実施されます。そのため今後のバージョンアップにより、本ブログで紹介する画面キャプチャや、操作が異なる可能性があることをご了承ください。（本ブログは2026年3月に作成しています。）

SAP Analytics Cloudにおける計算の追加

SAP Anaytics Cloudで計算式を追加する方法は、大きく以下の2つがあります。

  • モデルでの計算式追加
    計算式の定義場所をモデルで一元管理するため、複数のストーリーでモデルを利用した場合でも数字の整合性がとれ、統制のとれた計算の定義が可能
  • ストーリーでの計算式追加
    ストーリー作成時に新しい項目を任意に追加し、レポート上で利用することが可能であり、分析要件に柔軟に対応可能

今回は後者のストーリーでの計算式追加についてご紹介していきます。
ストーリーの編集モードで作成している際に、チャートに対して計算式を定義して新しい項目を追加することができます。計算には四則演算の演算子だけでなく、IF()やABS()などの関数や、ANDや>などの条件も使用することができます。さらには、平均値や累計値、差異などを定義するための設定もご用意しています。

詳細はヘルプページをご覧ください：チャートでのカスタム計算の作成 | SAP Help Portal

 

ストーリーでの計算式追加（営業利益率、累計売上高の計算）［ハンズオン］

0. はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成で作成したストーリーを編集モードで開こう！

0.1. ナビゲーションバーのファイルより作成したストーリーを開き、「編集」をクリックします。

0.2. 「Page_1」の右横の「v」をクリックし、「複製」をクリックしてページを複製します。

 

0.3. 「Page_1 (1)」の右横の「v」をクリックし、「タイトルの編集」をクリックし、タイトルを「計算」に変更します。

 

1. 営業利益率を計算させて表示しよう！

1.1. 数値ポイントチャートをクリックした上、右パネル（ビルダパネル）を開きます。

1.2. メジャー > 一次値 > 「＋メジャーの追加」をクリックします。

1.3. 「＋計算の追加」をクリックします。

1.4. 営業利益÷売上高の計算を追加します。タイプを「計算メジャー」を選択し、名前(ID)を「営業利益率」と入力します。式の編集欄で「[」と入力し、ポップアップで出てきたメジャーの候補から「営業利益」を選択します。

1.5. 続けて「/」を入力して、もう一度「[」と入力し、ポップアップで出てきたメジャーの候補から「売上高」を選択します。

1.6. 左下の「書式」をクリックし、「有効な式です。」と表示されることを確認します。「OK」をクリックします。

1.7. 書式を変更します。追加された営業利益率の「•••」をクリックし、「書式...」をクリックします。

1.8. 「基本のメジャーの単位を使用」のチェックを外してから 、スケールを「パーセント」に変更し、「OK」をクリックします。

1.9. 営業利益率が表示されました！

 

2. 累計売上高を計算させて表示させよう！

年度単位で累計値で計算させて、四半期ごとの累計を表示させてみよう！

 2.1. 売上高の棒チャートをクリックし、ビルダパネルにてメジャー > 売上高 > 「×」をクリックしてメジャーから外します。

2.2. メジャー > 「＋少なくとも1メジャーが必要です」をクリックします。

2.3. 「＋計算の追加」をクリックします。

2.4. 売上高を年度ごとに累計させる計算を作成します。タイプを「制限メジャー」と選択し、名前(ID)を「累計売上高」と入力します。メジャーを「売上高」、ディメンションを「月」、値を「ナビゲーション > 終了日付(累計) > 年度累計」を選択して「OK」をクリックします。
2.5. ディメンションが四半期ごとに設定されているため四半期ごとの累計が表示され、年度ごとに更新されていることが確認できます。

今回は計算の追加方法の紹介でした。SAP Analytics Cloudは他にも様々な表現や便利な機能があり、本ブログシリーズでご紹介しています。
はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - シリーズまとめ - SAP Communityからご参照ください。

entry.title: Introducing Advanced Commodity Risk Analytics (ACRA)
entry.content:
As part of our ongoing commitment to continuously improve SAP Commodity Management, we are excited to introduce Advanced Commodity Risk Analytics (ACRA),a next-generation product designed to meet the growing complexity and scale of modern commodity trading operations.

Commodity markets are evolving rapidly, with increasing data volumes, tighter reporting timelines, and greater demands for transparency and performance. ACRA is built to address these challenges head-on, providing a robust, future-ready foundation for commodity risk analytics.

What is ACRA?

Advanced Commodity Risk Analytics (ACRA) is a comprehensive risk analytics solution that reimagines how commodity risk is calculated, processed, and reported.

At the core of ACRA is a complete redesign of the existing Risk Analytics architecture. This redesign spans, over a series of releases, the entire analytics lifecycle—from commodity position management and mark-to-market (MtM) calculations to trading profit and loss (P&L) reporting. The result is a standardized, scalable risk analytics product capable of supporting the most demanding commodity trading environments.

Our objective is simple but ambitious: to deliver a high-performance, cloud-native Risk Analytics solution that can manage extensive data volumes while simplifying adoption and lowering the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for our customers.

Why ACRA? Key Benefits

ACRA is built with both today’s requirements and tomorrow’s needs in mind. Key benefits include:

Scalability

Analytical processes are decoupled from transactional processes. As a result, they are no longer constrained by the resources of the SAP S/4HANA transactional system. These analytical processes operate as a pure cloud application and can scale naturally.

High Performance

Risk calculation is divided into simplified stacks that can be processed simultaneously in parallel threads. Persisting calculation results delivers further enhancements to reporting performance.

Future-Proof Architecture

Using modern cloud architecture and an API-first open interface design, SAP, partners, and customers can easily introduce advanced risk analytics features and custom business logic.

Seamless Integration

Native integration exists between the transactional system SAP S/4HANA and the risk analytics platform. This eliminates the need for a separate custom interface or any side-car solutions.

Delivery Flexibility

The release cycles of a cloud application are independent of the SAP S/4HANA release cycles. This speed and flexibility enable faster innovation cycles for our customers accelerating benefit realisation.

Core Features Available Today

ACRA delivers powerful functionality right out of the gate, enabling organizations to modernize their commodity risk operations immediately.

Risk Calculation Engine

  • Introduce a tailored, modular risk calculation engine for Position Reporting
  • Perform only the necessary risk calculation steps, which shortens processing time and makes the calculation workflow easier to maintain and extend
  • Enable running multiple calculation tasks at the same time to speed up processing
  • Introduce a refined integration data model for SAP S/4HANA, which reduces redundant data storage and improves consistency between transactional data and risk results
  • Executes risk calculations in the cloud (outside the SAP S/4HANA core system), which frees up core system’s resources and avoids running duplicate calculations
  • Store calculated results in the cloud as the system of record, using a two-tier storage strategy – a fast ‘hot’ layer for recent data and a lower-cost ‘cold’ layer for historical archives – to optimize performance and costs

Snapshot and Scheduling Cockpit

  • Schedule regular activities such as market data quality checks, Position calculations, end-of-day and end-of-month result validation and day-over-day comparison.
  • Provide automation of routine tasks and a clear sequence and status of the workflow – Risk analysts receive notifications when any calculation step errors out and can access error logs for diagnosis to improve control by allowing key users (e.g. risk managers) to review and approve results in a dedicated workflow before the figures are published to all users and management.

Manage Market Curves

Market Curves are centrally managed and linked to:

  • Market Products
  • Market Locations
  • Risk Domains (such as profit centres or global regions assigned to company codes)

Each Market Curve contains relevant price indexes—such as Derivative Contracts and Basis IDs—ensuring that related risk is accurately calculated and reported across the organization.

ACRA supports complex market curve definitions – you can combine multiple price indices with custom weightings or adjustments (like basis differentials) as long as their date ranges don’t overlap, instead of having to create separate curves for each scenario.

Manage Maturity Calendars

Maturity Calendars are assigned to the Derivative Contract Specification (DCS) and enable:

  • Definition of the market norm/orthodoxy
  • Early market rolls
  • Default period derivation directly from SAP S/4HANA DCS settings

This ensures consistency between trading configurations and risk analytics while providing flexibility where required.

End-of-Day (EoD) Processing

ACRA integrates with SAP’s Advanced Financial Closing (AFC) to run a robust end-of-day process that prepares consolidated position reports

This provides flexibility through:

  • Sequencing pre-delivered risk calculation steps for commodity risk calculations (Derivatives and Logistics)
  • custom processing steps to address company-specific requirements (e.g., proprietary calculations or data validation checks)
  • Include manual verification steps (for example, requiring a manager’s sign-off) to ensure accuracy before proceeding.

The result is a controlled, auditable, and flexible EoD process.

Consolidated Position End of Day Reporting

Using SAP Datasphere data models and analytical clients such as SAP Analytics Cloud, ACRA delivers comprehensive risk reporting, from high-level summaries down to individual commodity contracts and inventory positions.

Users gain full visibility into their End-of-Day positions with drill-down capabilities that support better decision-making and governance.

Conclusion - A Modern Foundation for Commodity Risk

ACRA represents a significant step forward in commodity risk analytics—combining performance, scalability, and a future-proof design into a single standardized solution. By modernizing the architecture and simplifying adoption, ACRA empowers commodity trading organizations to manage risk more effectively today while staying ready for what’s next.

Visit SAP Help Portal for more an Administration Guide: 

https://help.sap.com/docs/CCS/7b6b503b6ae0446fb3d6801c98f56b1e/349e9247507644e8a8df8a20cb60af8b.html?locale=en-US

Stay tuned for future innovations by following SAP Roadmap Explorer  for Commodity Management: 

https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?PRODUCT=42F2E964F4E71EDAA6A3587248CD40E5&range=CURRENT-LAST

entry.title: はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - デザインのTips
entry.content:
本ブログシリーズはSAP Analytics CloudのBI機能を使ってダッシュボードやレポートの作成を行う方を対象とし、SAP Analytics CloudのBIにおける基本の操作やTipsをハンズオン形式でご紹介します。

本ブログでは、ストーリー作成時に役立つデザインのTipsをいくつかご紹介します。

 

目次：

  • ロゴ画像を挿入してみよう！
  • 背景色を変えてみよう！
  • 複数のウィジェットを一つのパネルにまとめてみよう！
  • ウィジェットの位置や大きさを細かく調整しよう！（キャンバスレイアウト）
  • チャートを綺麗に整列させよう！
  • オリジナルのカラーパレットを作成しよう！
  • （参考）完成したストーリーをテンプレートとして保存しよう！

※ SAP Analytics Cloudは、四半期に１度のペースで製品のバージョンアップが実施されます。そのため今後のバージョンアップにより、本ブログで紹介する画面キャプチャや、操作が異なる可能性があることをご了承ください。（本ブログは2026年3月に作成しています。）

 

ロゴ画像を挿入してみよう！

ストーリーに画像を挿入することができます。会社のロゴなどを入れてみましょう！

1. 挿入 > ＋ > その他 > 画像をクリックします。

2. 「＋画像の追加」をクリックし、任意の画像を選択します。

画像を挿入することができました！

 

背景色を変えてみよう！

ストーリーの背景の色を好きな色に変更してみましょう！

1. 右パネル（スタイルパネル）を開き、ストーリーの余白をクリックします。キャンバス > 背景色の「ｖ」をクリックします。

2. 好きな色を選び、「OK」をクリックします。

3. （オプション）各ウィジェットの背景色を変更します。（デフォルトでは色なしのため背景と同化してしまいます）
ウィジェットをクリックし（Shift＋クリックで複数選択でもOK）、ウィジェット > 背景色の「ｖ」をクリックして色を変更します。

背景色を変更することができました！


複数のウィジェットを一つのパネルにまとめてみよう！

パネルを挿入して複数のウィジェットをまとめてスッキリさせてみましょう！

1. 挿入 > ＋ > コンテナ > パネルをクリックします。
2. 任意のウィジェットを挿入されたパネルにドラッグ＆ドロップします。

パネルの背景色を変えて位置を調整することで、複数のウィジェットを一つのエリアにまとめることができました！

 

ウィジェットの位置や大きさを細かく調整しよう！（キャンバスレイアウト）

キャンバスレイアウトでストーリーを作成する際、マウスを使ってウィジェットの角で調整するだけでなく、ウィジェットの位置や大きさをピクセル単位で細かく調整することができます。

1. 右パネル（スタイルパネル）を開き、任意のウィジェットをクリックします。サイズと位置でピクセル単位で変更することができます。

 


2. （オプション）ピクセル単位ではなく、パーセントで設定することも可能です。パーセントで設定すると、ストーリーを開く画面の画素数が変更されてもウィジェットがそれに伴って大きさが変わり、画面いっぱいに表示することができておすすめです。（全てのオブジェクトをパーセントで設定する必要があります）

イメージ：

 3. （オプション）ウィジェットの角を丸くすることができます。スタイルパネルのウィジェット > 枠線を「すべての枠線」に変更し、コーナー半径を任意の値に変更します。

チャートを綺麗に整列させよう！

散らばっている複数のウィジェットを一気に整列させてみましょう！

1. ウィジェットをShift＋クリックで複数選択肢、「•••」をクリックし、ウジェットを整列より任意の操作を選択します。
ウィジェットが綺麗に整列しました！
 

オリジナルのカラーパレットを作成しよう！

チャートの色を好きな色で表示するオリジナルのカラーパレットを活用してみましょう！企業のイメージカラーに統一することでよりオリジナリティのあるストーリーを作成することができます。

1. 右パネル（ビルダパネル）を開き、チャートをクリックします。色内のデフォルトで設定されているパレットの鉛筆マークをクリックします。

2. ストーリーパレットの「＋」をクリックします

3. 任意の色を設定し、「保存」をクリックします。 
オリジナルのカラーパレットで表示できました！

（参考）完成したストーリーをテンプレートとして保存しよう！

綺麗に整えたストーリーをベースとして他のストーリーを作成できるようにしたい場合、テンプレートとして保存しておくことで効率的にストーリーを作成することができます。

1. ファイル > 保存 > テンプレートとして保存... をクリックします。

2.  任意の名前を入力して「保存」をクリックします。

3. 保存したテンプレートからストーリーを作成してみます。ナビゲーションバー > ストーリー > テンプレート より保存したテンプレートをクリックします。

テンプレートを活用してストーリーの作成ができるようになりました！

 

今回はデザインのTipsの紹介でした。SAP Analytics Cloudは他にも様々な表現や便利な機能があり、本ブログシリーズでご紹介しています。
はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - シリーズまとめ - SAP Communityからご参照ください。

entry.title: Two Ways to Build Agentic Reporting in SAP: Python Agent on SAP BTP vs. Joule Studio Agent
entry.content:
1. Introduction

In my previous blog post on Agentic Reporting, I talked about how modern reporting goes beyond dashboards — it requires systems that reason, explain, and act.

In this follow‑up, instead of going straight into a specific implementation, I want to answer a bigger question:

What are the different ways to build an AI Agent in the SAP ecosystem?

With SAP’s evolving AI landscape, you now have two powerful paths to build enterprise‑grade agents:

Option A — Build a Python Agent on SAP BTP

Custom, full‑code, flexible Agent service that integrates with SAP Datasphere via SQL or OData.

Option B — Build an Agent directly in SAP Joule Studio

A no‑code/low‑code agent builder inside SAP Build for reasoning, orchestration, and multi‑system actions.

Both options are valid. Both can support Agentic Reporting. But they differ significantly in architecture, flexibility, governance, and effort.

This post compares both, so you can choose the best path for your use case.

2. The Two Agent‑Building Paths Explained

Option A — Python Agent on SAP BTP (Full‑Code + Maximum Flexibility)

This is the classic approach where you build an application in Python, deploy it on SAP BTP (Cloud Foundry or Kyma), and connect it to SAP Datasphere.

When is this ideal?

  • Heavy data processing
  • Custom ML/AI logic
  • Integration with complex non-SAP systems
  • You want maximum control over logic, dependencies, libraries
  • Advanced reasoning models + custom frameworks
  • You want to host an API that other tools can call (including Joule)

What your Python Agent can do

  • Connect to SAP Datasphere using:
    • SQL (hdbcli)
    • OData Analytical or Relational APIs
  • Execute complex logic (anomaly detection, simulations, scoring)
  • Write data back to Open SQL schema
  • Expose REST endpoints for other SAP systems and Joule agents to consume

Architecture Summary

  • Python app on BTP
  • Service bindings → HANA Cloud credentials via VCAP_SERVICES
  • OAuth → Datasphere OData access
  • HANA Cloud SQL → read/write via Open SQL schema
  • Python performs reasoning, actions, decisions

Option B — Joule Studio Agent (SAP-native no‑code Agent Builder)

SAP Joule Studio allows business teams and architects to build agents without needing to write any code.

These agents can:

  • Plan
  • Reason
  • Execute workflows
  • Call APIs (via Joule Skills)
  • Use SAP Knowledge Graph
  • Use Document Grounding
  • Orchestrate SAP and non‑SAP tools

When is this ideal?

  • You want an SAP-native, governed agent
  • You want quick setup (no DevOps, no runtime)
  • You want conversational + actionable agents
  • You want orchestration across SAP applications
  • You want to avoid managing Python runtimes
  • You want automatic reasoning and traceability

What your Joule Agent can do

  • Connect to SAP systems via destinations
  • Invoke Joule Skills (API calls, BTP actions)
  • Consume Datasphere data via OData APIs
  • Perform reasoning using SAP’s LLMs
  • Automate workflows in SuccessFactors, S/4HANA, Ariba, etc.

Architecture Summary

  • Joule Studio = Agent Builder
  • Uses Destinations for integration
  • Uses Skills & Actions for API calls
  • LLM-powered agentic reasoning

No deploy, no runtime maintenance

3. Comparing the Two Approaches

4. Which Option Should You Use?

When to Choose Python Agent on BTP

Technical Complexity Requirements:

  • Advanced algorithmic logic - When you need custom algorithms beyond standard business logic
  • Heavy transformations/ETL - For complex data processing workflows requiring full programmatic control 
  • External ML libraries - Integration with sklearn, PyTorch, transformers, or other specialized libraries 

Integration Scenarios:

  • API endpoint creation - When other systems need to call your agent as a service
  • Any system connectivity - Leveraging the "any API, any system" capability for non-SAP integrations 

When to Choose Joule Studio Agent

Rapid Development Needs:

  • Fast deployment with the "lower effort, no-code" approach 
  • Built-in agentic reasoning eliminating custom development overhead 

Enterprise Governance:

  • SAP-native governance & logging for compliance and auditability 
  • Built-in trace, executions, reasoning view for operational transparency 
  • Skill marketplace access for pre-built capabilities 

SAP Ecosystem Integration:

  • SAP-first integrations with S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, and Ariba 
  • SAP Knowledge Graph + Documents for automatic grounding 
  • Workflow automation & orchestration as the primary use case

5. Conclusion:

SAP’s Agentic AI is evolving rapidly, and you now have two mature ways of building enterprise‑grade agents:

1. Python Agent on SAP BTP

Flexible, powerful, code-based, perfect for analytics and complex logic.

2️. Joule Studio Agent

SAP-native, governed, no-code, perfect for orchestration, reasoning, and functional automation.

Both are valid choices and can even work together.

 

Next in the series:

  • Blog #3: How to Build a Python Agent for Agentic Reporting (Datasphere + BTP)
  • Blog #4: How to Build an SAP-native Joule Agent for Agentic Reporting

Previous Blog in the series:

Blog #1: Agentic Reporting - The Next Evolution of SAP Reporting

entry.title: SAP 2026: Between standard power and individual competitive advantage
entry.content:
The world of SAP SE is changing. While SAP stock and the SAP dividend dominate the headlines of the financial press, companies are facing a much deeper question: How much standardization is necessary and how much individuality is permissible?

At Toraman Consulting, we follow a clear principle: The SAP standard is the foundation, but we don't condemn in-house developments . It's about maintaining a balance.

The foundation: Why the standard is more important today than ever

With the rise of SAP Cloud and innovations like Joule (SAP's new AI assistant), a clean core is becoming essential. Those who are too deeply entrenched in the system code of the old SAP R/3 world cannot fully leverage the advantages of S/4HANA and the new Joule agents . The standard ensures stability, update capability, and access to state-of-the-art functions in modules such as SAP MM , SAP SD , and SAP PP .

The optional part: Where in-house developments make the difference

But we also know that a company isn't successful simply by doing the exact same thing as everyone else. Real competitive advantages often lie in processes that are anything but run-of-the-mill.

Our philosophy at Toraman Consulting :

  • Weighing options instead of rejecting: We carefully examine where the standard is sufficient and where a targeted extension provides the decisive added value.
  • Securing advantages: If an in-house development strengthens your market position or makes your customers happier, then we will defend this solution.
  • Modern Development: We use the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) to create individuality without contaminating the digital core.

Partnerships and Market Trends 2026

The market is responding to this need for expert knowledge. Large consulting firms like KPMG and Deloitte are heavily investing in strategic alliances to bridge the gap between standard software and customized AI solutions. As a partner in the SAP Open Ecosystem, we offer you precisely this flexibility: the security of a global market leader combined with the personalized support your business requires.

Conclusion: Balance wins

Whether you're building your SAP systems from scratch or need a complex migration strategy, let's discuss your options together. The goal is a system that's stable enough for the future, yet flexible enough to reflect your unique strengths.

entry.title: Integrating SAP Databricks with SAP CPQ and SAP Datasphere for Analytics & Reporting
entry.content:
In today’s data-driven enterprises, sales teams rely on real-time insights to make faster and smarter decisions. Yet, many organizations still struggle with fragmented data landscapes—especially when dealing with complex systems like SAP CPQ (Configure Price Quote), SAP Datasphere, and advanced analytics platforms.

To solve this challenge, we designed an end‑to‑end architecture that seamlessly connects SAP CPQ, Databricks, and SAP Datasphere. The result? A unified, governed, and scalable analytics ecosystem that unlocks deeper sales insights.

This blog breaks down the business goals, architecture, and implementation blueprint behind the integration.

Birds High View. (Process View)

Why This Integration Matters

The core objective is simple but powerful:
Bring SAP CPQ quotation data into SAP Datasphere—through Databricks—to enable centralized reporting, analytics, and visualization.

This pipeline creates:

  • A single source of truth for CPQ data
  • Real‑time or near‑real‑time insights
  • A governed, secure, and scalable data platform
  • Smooth interoperability across SAP and non‑SAP systems

Business Requirements at a Glance

Functional Requirements

To support modern analytics use cases, the solution must:

  • Extract all sales quotation records from SAP CPQ via APIs
  • Store data in a governed, versioned Delta Lake format
  • Share data securely with SAP Datasphere
  • Enable full data lineage and governance
  • Support real-time or near-real-time use cases

Non‑Functional Requirements

Behind the scenes, the platform must also ensure:

  • Performance: Process incremental CPQ changes with <5‑minute latency
  • Security: Encryption and role‑based access control throughout
  • Scalability: Handle 100,000+ quotations per day
  • Compliance: GDPR and SOX adherence for sensitive financial data

Solution Architecture Overview

The integration spans four major layers: data sources, data platform, data sharing, and data consumption.

1. Data Sources

The journey begins with SAP CPQ, which exposes:

  • REST APIs for extracting quotation data
  • OAuth 2.0 authentication using client credentials

These APIs provide structured access to all quotation objects and line items.

2. Databricks – The Data Transformation Engine

Databricks acts as the data engineering and governance hub.

  • PySpark ETL notebooks orchestrate ingestion and transformation
  • Unity Catalog oversees centralized governance
  • Delta Lake ensures ACID transactions and version-controlled storage

This allows teams to build clean, enriched, analytics-ready datasets.

3. Data Sharing: Delta Sharing + SAP BDC

For secure, open data sharing, the architecture leverages:

  • Delta Sharing Protocol for exchanging datasets using industry standards
  • SAP BDC (Business Data Connectivity) for ORD-based integration into Datasphere

The combination ensures that SAP Datasphere receives fresh, trusted data with minimal friction.

4. Consumption Layer

Finally, business users analyze the curated CPQ data through:

  • SAP Datasphere for modeling and semantic layering
  • SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) for dashboards and reports

The result is a seamless analytics experience with governed enterprise data.

The Data Model: What’s Being Shared?

To enable deep sales analytics, the shared data model includes:

Key Entities

  • Quotations: Quote ID, Quote Status, Opportunity ID, Customer ID, Amount, Status, Quote Created Date
  • Line Items: Product ID, Quantity, Unit Price, Net Price, Target Price, Margins
  • Customers: Business Partners,  Region, Segment
  • Products: Product Name, Category, SKU

Relationships

  • Quotation → Line Items: One-to-many
  • Quotation → Customer: Many-to-one

This structure supports reporting such as win rate trends, regional sales patterns, product profitability, and more.

Integration Flows

1. Data Ingestion 

  • Frequency: Hourly incremental loads
  • One Example API Endpoint: /api/v1/quotes
  • Resilience: Retry logic with exponential backoff

2. Data Transformation in Databricks

Transformations include:

  • Cleansing and deduplication
  • Enriching quotations with master data
  • Creating derived metrics like:
    • Win Rate
    • Average Deal Size
    • Quote-to-close duration

3. Data Governance

To meet compliance and governance needs:

  • PII fields are tagged and masked
  • Row-level security applies by sales region
  • All schema changes are version controlled through Delta Lake

Security & Compliance Framework

The pipeline is designed with enterprise-grade security:

  • Authentication: Service principals for API access
  • Authorization: Fine-grained ACLs via Unity Catalog
  • Encryption: TLS for in-transit, AES‑256 for at-rest data
  • Auditability: Complete logging of data access and transformations

This ensures audit readiness for GDPR, SOX, and internal IT security checks.

How We Measure Success

To validate business adoption and technical reliability, we track:

Data Quality

99% accuracy of quotation data

Platform Availability

  • 99.9% uptime SLA for the pipeline

User Adoption

  • 50+ monthly active business users in SAP Analytics Cloud

entry.title: Why Attend The Fabric of Data & AI: Insights from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone & More
entry.content:
JOIN US AT THE FABRIC OF DATA & AI ON MARCH 24, 2026, 11AM EDT | 11AM CET | 1PM SGT

AI without context is like walking to the car wash.  

You may have seen the viral video: someone asks ChatGPT whether to walk or drive to the car wash. AI analyzes distance, time, and efficiency, then recommends walking. It misses the obvious: you need the car there to wash it. 

It's funny, until you realize the same disconnect is happening in business decisions every day. AI can analyze data, optimize metrics, and execute quickly, but without understanding business context, it can provide technically correct answers that completely miss the mark. 

As organizations deploy AI agents, intelligent applications, and large language models, many are experiencing a critical gap: the data strategies that powered the last decade of innovation weren't designed for autonomous decision-making. 

The Fabric of Data & AI, a virtual summit on March 24, 2026, brings together industry leaders at the forefront of this transformation. Join Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer of SAP Data & Analytics, in conversation with leaders from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone, Team Liquid, Mead Johnson Nutrition, and the Open Data Institute. 

Here are three reasons you should attend. 

1. Understand why yesterday’s data strategies fall short 

For decades, data strategies have focused on extracting data into centralized systems for applications and dashboards. This approach served its purpose for traditional analytics, but it created a problem: the loss of important business context. 

When AI makes critical decisions, such as allocating inventory, prioritizing customers, and managing cash flow, it needs more than just accurate data. It needs to grasp the business relationships, priorities, and trade-offs that determine the right course of action. 

At this summit, you'll discover: 

  • What happens when business context gets lost as data moves across systems 
  • Why AI agents can optimize individually but create problems collectively 
  • How preserving business meaning by design creates a competitive advantage 

2. Learn from leaders solving the same challenges you face

You’ll hear directly from leaders who are building data and AI strategies in their organizations, including: 

  • Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer, SAP Data & Analytics 
  • Jannie Affeld, VP Finance Systems and ERP, Google 
  • Malin Persson, CIO & Head of Enterprise IT, Ericsson 
  • Ricard Rovira Marti, Head of Corporate IT Platforms, Vodafone 
  • Steve Arhancet, Co-CEO and Co-Owner, Team Liquid 
  • Ganesh Sivakumar, Global Chief Data Analytics & AI Officer, Mead Johnson Nutrition 
  • Louise Burke, Chief Executive Officer, Open Data Institute 

Each brings different approaches, unique challenges, and proven outcomes from real-world experience across finance, telecommunications, technology, esports, and consumer goods. 

3. Gain actionable insights for your organization 

Develop a strategic perspective on the decisions that determine AI success. Join us for: 

  • Clarity on the shift: Discover why shared organizational knowledge is becoming a structural requirement for AI success. 
  • The architectural framework: Learn how a business data fabric connects data, processes, and business rules to keep AI grounded in business context. 
  • The integration model: See how technology, process, and culture must evolve together, and why all three are essential for success. 

Whether you lead data and analytics, drive business strategy, or oversee technology implementation, this event provides the insights to successfully navigate this transition. 

Save your spot 

The conversation about data and AI is everywhere, but most of it focuses on what AI can do, not what it needs to succeed. Don't miss this opportunity to learn from the leaders who are building the foundation for reliable AI. 

Register now for The Fabric of Data & AI and join the conversation on March 24, 2026.

entry.title: はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - データアナライザ
entry.content:
本ブログシリーズはSAP Analytics CloudのBI機能をつかってダッシュボードやレポートの作成を行う方を対象とし、SAP Analytics CloudのBIにおける基本の操作やTipsをハンズオン形式でご紹介します。

本ブログでは、「ストーリーを見ている時にグラフやテーブルに表示されていない分析軸を含めたより詳細な情報や差異の要因を知りたい」際に分析軸や分析対象の数値項目を追加したり入れ替えたりしながらアドホック分析を行う機能である、データアナライザについてご紹介いたします。

 

目次：

  • データアナライザの概要
  • データアナライザの利用方法[ハンズオン]
    • データアナライザの有効化
    • データアナライザを利用した分析
    • インサイトをストーリーに固定：分析内容をストーリー上にピン留めしてすぐにアクセスできるように設定

※ベースとなるサンプルストーリーの作成⼿順は、はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成 - SAP Communityでご紹介しています。
※ SAP Analytics Cloudは、四半期に１度のペースで製品のバージョンアップが実施されます。そのため今後のバージョンアップにより、本ブログで紹介する画⾯キャプチャや、操作が異なる可能性があることをご了承ください。（本ブログは2026年3⽉に作成しています。）

データアナライザの概要

SAP Analytics Cloudのデータアナライザ機能は、分析軸や分析対象の数値項目を追加したり入れ替えたりしながらアドホック分析を行うことで、分析を深めたい部分に関してのチャートやテーブルを追加で作成することなく、ユーザーが即座にアドホックに詳細分析を行うことに役立ちます。

詳しくはHelp Portalもご参照ください：データアナライザ

※データアナライザはストーリーからだけでなく、機能メニューやファイルリポジトリ、JustAsk機能などから利用することも可能です。本ブログではストーリーからの起動についてご紹介します。詳細はHelp Portalをご確認ください： データアナライザの起動

データアナライザの利用方法

0. はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成 - SAP Communityで作成したストーリーを編集モードで開こう！

0.1. ストーリーのナビゲーションバーから「編集」をクリックします。

 

1. データアナライザを有効化しよう！

1.1. 対象のチャートまたはテーブルをクリックし、右パネル（ビルダパネル）を開きます。

 

1.2 パネルメニューをスクロールダウンし、「チャートのプロパティ」＞「表示モード」から「データアナライザを有効化」にチェックをつけます。

※チャートやテーブルからのデータアナライザの利用設定はストーリー設定から一括で有効化することも可能です。一括で有効化したい場合には「ストーリー設定」＞「ストーリー詳細」＞「データ探索の設定」を有効化します。

 

1.3. ストーリーを保存し、ストーリーを「表示モード」に切り替えます。

 

2. データアナライザを使って分析しよう！

2.1. 詳細に分析したいチャートまたはテーブルをクリックし、メニューから「データアナライザを開く」を選択します。（「現在のタブで開く」「新規タブで開く」は任意に選択ください）

 

2.2. データアナライザが開かれます。画面には2.1で選択したチャートのデータが表示されています。メニューバーのフィルタボタンを押すと、ストーリーのチャートで利用されていたフィルタが引き継がれていることが確認できます。

 2.3. 画面右の「≪（展開）」ボタンをクリックし、ビルダパネルを開きます。

 

2.4. 追加したい項目を「利用可能なオブジェクト」から行または列のアイコンをクリックするかドラッグ＆ドロップで「ビルダ」に追加し、ディメンションやメジャーを追加します。（今回は例として「製品グループ」を行に追加します）

 2.5. 2.4の操作を繰り返しながら分析を行います。行に配置された項目を列にドラッグ＆ドロップで移動したり、項目の並び順を入れ替えることも可能です。また、「表示タイプ」からチャートでの表示に切り替えることも可能です。

操作についての 詳細はHelp Portalもご参照ください：データアナライザ

 

3. データアナライザの分析内容をストーリー上にピン留めしてすぐにアクセスできるように設定

をしよう！

データアナライザのインサイトをストーリーに固定することでデータアナライザの分析内容をストーリー上にピン留めしてすぐにアクセスできるように設定することが可能です。作成したインサイトに直接アクセスすることができるため分析を効率化することができます。

3.1. メニューバーから「保存」を選択します。保存ダイアログで、インサイトの名前を追加し、「保存してストーリーに固定」を選択します。※ストーリーの「表示」モードからデータアナライザを起動する必要があります。

 

3.2 ストーリーに戻り、チャートまたはテーブルをクリックし、メニューから「データアナライザを開く」を選択します。固定されたインサイトが表示されていることが確認できます。固定されたインサイトを選択し、3.1で保存したインサイトに直接アクセスできます。

 

今回はデータアナライザ 機能のご紹介でした。SAP Analytics Cloudは他にも様々な表現や便利な機能があり、本ブログシリーズでご紹介しています。はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - シリーズまとめ - SAP Communityからご参照ください。

entry.title: Blog 3 - From BW 7.5 to SAP BDC: Reusing Extractors, Custom Logic, and Models Standard Extractors
entry.content:
This is part 3 of our blog series investigating how to reuse existing BW artefacts in BDC such as extractors and custom logic.

As organizations embrace cloud-native data architectures, the journey from SAP BW on HANA 7.5 to SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) represents a critical modernization path. Our team recently completed a pilot implementation focusing on first, lifting BW into SAP BDC and modernizing it using SAP Datasphere as the primary target architecture.

This blog post shares practical insights and key learnings from this experience, particularly around handling standard extractors and preserving custom extractors business logic in SAP BDC.

I. The Challenge: Standard Extractors in a Modern Architecture

Think of standard SAP extractors as old filing systems that worked perfectly in traditional warehouses but struggle in modern cloud environments. Standard SAP extractors (2LIS_02_ITM, 0CO_OM_CCA_9, 2LIS_03_BF) present significant migration challenges when moving from traditional BW environments to SAP Business Data Cloud. This was primarily due to missing primary key definitions. SAP has modernized these extractors through SAP Datasphere, which acts as an intelligent intermediary that handles the complex transformation from legacy extraction patterns to cloud-native data products, enabling faster, more reliable data flows without requiring extensive custom coding.

Implementation Reality

With delta processing already active and productive in your Datasphere environment, the three critical extractors now operate seamlessly: 2LIS_02_ITM delivers purchase order changes, 0CO_OM_CCA_9 streams cost center postings, and 2LIS_03_BF provides material movements for non-cumulative inventory analysis. Datasphere eliminates traditional complexity by managing initialization, delta processing, and data semantics automatically, transforming raw SAP tables into business-ready entities (Purchase Orders, Cost Centers, Stock) that SAP Analytics Cloud can consume directly for analytics and planning scenarios. The same entities can also by consumed directly by SAP HANA Cloud for custom AI / ML scenarios or by end-to-end Intelligent Applications.

Design implications now that delta handling works

With delta mode active in Datasphere, your design simplifies and becomes more “cloud-native”:

  1. Each extractor follows a clear pattern:
  • Run initialization and delta in Replication Flow in Datasphere

 

  • Configure the extractor “primary key” in the Configure schema

 

  • Enable scheduled delta loads (e.g., hourly, daily, near real time).

 

  • New data is created in your source system (ECC or S4)

 

  • Deltas are replicated in Datasphere (table is filtered for the specific PO that was newly created)
  1. Operational benefits:
  • No more large daily full loads.
  • Network and storage usage is reduced because only changes move.
  • Latency improves: BDC can be updated much more frequently.
  • Monitoring focuses on delta queues and extractor status in Datasphere.

Practical checklist for your migration

When running this in a real project with delta fully available:

  • Per extractor:
    • Confirm that initialization has been done successfully in Datasphere.
    • Ensure delta jobs are configured, scheduled, and monitored.
    • Align delta frequency with business needs (close to real time vs batch).
  • In Datasphere:
    • Model business keys clearly (e.g., PO number, item, company code; Controlling area, cost center, period).
    • Build semantic models (views, business entities) that hide technical complexity from BDC consumers.Stay tuned for more details on this topic in the next blog post.
    • Use lineage so others know which extractors feed which BDC data products.
  • In BDC:
    • Point your analytical models and stories to the Datasphere-based artifacts instead of direct raw tables.
    • Design calculations assuming data is continuously refreshed via delta.

Beyond standard extractors, the real differentiation in a BDC migration comes from how you bring your custom fields and business logic in extractor enhancements into the new landscape.

 

II. How to Handle Custom Fields and Extractor Extensions in SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Datasphere

Reusing a customer’s existing custom logic is essential in SAP because it preserves years of in-house business knowledge, avoids costly redevelopment, and accelerates modernization while keeping results from business outcomes without business disruption.

In our pilot, we showed how SAP Datasphere within SAP Business Data Cloud can consume SAP standard extractors enhanced by custom fields without redesigning the entire data flow from scratch. Using the 0FI_AP_4 extractor (which supports both full and delta loads) as an example, the steps below illustrate how to expose customized business rules in financial data end‑to‑end to BDC.

These steps are not meant to be a full tutorial on how to enhance a standard SAP extractor – that pattern has been around for decades – but we believe it is still useful to summarize the key activities here. We will also show how these enhanced extractors are replicated (initial load and delta) into SAP Datasphere, in SAP Business Data Cloud.

  1. Check Existing Fields and Enhancement Options
    1. In the source system, use RSA2 (or R0S2/RSA6 depending on release) to display DataSource 0FI_AP_4.
    2. Verify whether the fields you need are already present but hidden (this is common in standard extractors).
    3. If they are not available, plan to add them via an append structure and fill them using RSAP0001 or a BAdI implementation.
  2. Enhance the Extract Structure (Append Structure)
    1. Still in RSA2, navigate to the extract structure of 0FI_AP_4.
    2. Create an append structure and add your custom fields (usually prefixed with ZZ or YY).
    3. Assign correct data elements (taken from SE11 table definitions) and activate the append structure.

After activation, the new fields are physically part of the extract structure but not yet filled with data.

  1. Implement the Extractor Logic (User Exit / BAdI)
    1. Create or reuse an enhancement project in CMOD and assign enhancement RSAP0001.
    2. For transactional data, implement component EXIT_SAPLRSAP_001 (include ZXRSAU01) and write the ABAP logic that populates the new fields for DataSource 0FI_AP_4.
      • Use I_DATASOURCE in the exit to branch specifically on 0FI_AP_4.
      • Read the necessary tables (e.g. BSEG, BKPF or customer tables) with the SELECT statement and loop over the extraction internal table to fill the custom fields using field symbols.
    3. Activate the enhancement project and the include program.

(Alternative: in newer systems you can use the dedicated BAdI for DataSource enhancement instead of RSAP0001, the principle for the logic remains the same.)

  1. Unhide and Configure the Fields in RSA6
    1. Open RSA6 and choose the source system.
    2. Select DataSource 0FI_AP_4 in change mode.
    3. Locate the new fields and remove the Hide flag; if needed, adjust properties such as “field only known in exit”.
    4. Save and activate the DataSource so that the new fields become visible for extraction (RSA3) and for replication to BW/Datasphere.

If you extended an LO Cockpit DataSource, you might also need to check the extract structure and update/activate it via LBWE so that queue filling works correctly.

In our pilot, we have created a simple logic by assigning a single value (1000) to the controlling area.

  1. Replicate and Consume in SAP Datasphere within SAP BDC
    1. In SAP Datasphere, connect to the source system and activate the enhanced extractor 0FI_AP_4.
    2. Create a replication flow to run an initial full load and delta loads, now including the custom fields.
    3. Once the replication flow runs and creates the local table in Datasphere, the enhanced field has been replicated with its data automaticaly replicated.

This completes the chain from technical enhancement in S/4HANA (or ECC) to business‑ready data, analytics and AI in BDC, while following the standard BW enhancement process.

III. Next Steps: From data replication to answering business questions

Once extractors (standard and extended) are replicated into SAP Datasphere and exposed to BDC, the next step is to turn raw data into multi-dimensional models to provide data for analytical purposes to answer different business questions.

  1. In SAP Datasphere, build Analytic Models on top of the replicated tables and datasources, defining measures, dimensions and hierarchies that fulfill business requirements. The latest update on the data product generator allows to semantically import BW 7.5 PCE InfoProvider data (works like semantic onboarding but is triggered from BW side and pushed into the object store of SAP Datasphere).
  2. Publish these models as consumption artifacts (expose analytic models/views, create and share data products) that can be consumed by SAP Analytics Cloud , SAP HANA Cloud apps and other BDC clients, tools and applications.
  3. In SAP Analytics Cloud, create stories, dashboards, and planning models that leverage the Datasphere models for live reporting, seamless planning and simulation.
  4. In SAP HANA Cloud, build high performance end-to-end intelligent applications that leverage custom AI / ML and multi-model capabilities for structured and unstructured data.

This way, the project does not stop at data replication: it delivers an end‑to‑end analytical platform where business users consume trusted, delta‑enabled data products in SAP Business Data Cloud for fact based decision support.

Conclusion

Our pilot project demonstrated that migrating from SAP BW7.5 on HANA to SAP Business Data Cloud using SAP Datasphere is not only feasible but can be done while preserving critical business logic and custom investments.

The key to success lies in thorough planning, understanding the current state of your extraction landscape, and leveraging the migration as an opportunity to modernize while preserving what works. As SAP continues to enhance BDC capabilities, organizations can confidently plan their migration journeys knowing that both standard and custom extraction scenarios are well-supported.

Stay tuned for the 4th blog post in this series, ‘Beyond Data Warehousing, AI-assisted migration of Business Logic from BW 7.5 to BDC Part 1’

entry.title: Migrating to Seamless Planning in SAP Analytics Cloud: Steps, Pitfalls, and Workarounds
entry.content:
1️⃣Introduction – Why Move to Seamless Planning?

Migrating from SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Dedicated to Seamless Planning in SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) is not simply a transport exercise — it is an architectural shift.

Seamless Planning changes where and how your planning models are persisted and calculated. Instead of storing and calculating planning data in the SAC tenant itself, models can be deployed to SAP Datasphere, leveraging HANA Cloud memory allocated to that Datasphere tenant.

Strategic Advantages of Seamless Planning

  • Dedicated HANA Cloud memory allocation through SAP Datasphere, allowing planning workloads to leverage scalable enterprise infrastructure.
  • Alignment with the broader SAP Business Data Cloud architecture, enabling tighter integration between planning and enterprise data products.
  • Integration with both SAP-managed and custom Data Products, allowing planning models to consume and interact with governed enterprise data assets.
  • Support for Live Version capabilities, enabling direct integration with operational systems such as SAP S/4HANA.
  • Reduced duplication of plan and actual data by enabling hybrid architectures that combine planning persistence with live operational data.

Perhaps the most important concept to understand is this:

SAP Analytics Cloud supports hybrid persistence strategies.

You do not need to migrate every model in your tenant.

High-priority enterprise planning models can be deployed to SAP Datasphere (Seamless Planning) for dedicated memory and enterprise-grade architecture. Personal models, prototypes, or lower-priority planning models can continue leveraging shared resources in the public SAC tenant.

This means migration can be selective and strategic — not all-or-nothing.

2️⃣Migrating Using the Content Network (The Only Supported Method)

As of Q4 2025, the only supported way to convert an existing model to Seamless Planning — meaning switch the persistence layer from SAC to SAP Datasphere — is via the Content Network.

There is currently no migration wizard.

The persistence flag can only be changed during import.

Important Caveats Before You Begin

Before exporting or deleting anything, consider the following carefully.

Clear References on the Destination Tenant

Before importing the Content Network package, ensure that the destination tenant does not already contain models or public dimensions with the same technical identifiers.

If these objects already exist, the import will not prompt you to change the persistence layer. Any conflicting models or dimensions must therefore be deleted from the destination tenant before the package can be successfully imported.

Because of this requirement, many organizations find it simpler to migrate into a newly created, clean BDC tenant rather than attempting to convert an existing tenant that contains overlapping content.

Read the Section on Comments Migration Before Deleting Anything

If you are planning to delete models from an existing tenant, it is strongly recommended that you first review the Handling Comments section of this blog.

In some migration scenarios, comments may need to be backed up from a Production tenant to a Dev/Test tenant before the migration occurs, and then migrated again from Dev/Test into the new Seamless Planning tenant.

Deleting models without first planning for comment migration can result in permanent loss of historical planning discussions.

Back Up Data Before Deleting

If your Content Network package cannot include data due to size limitations, it is important to plan your data backup strategy before deleting any models.

Common approaches include creating a temporary copy of the model before deleting the original, exporting the data using the SAC Data Export API, or planning an OData or API-based reload process in advance.

Once the model has been deleted, restoring the associated data becomes significantly more complex.

Two SAC Tenants Are Required

If:

  • Your data volume exceeds Content Network limits
  • You must preserve comments

In these scenarios, a single-tenant migration is not feasible.

You will need:

  1. A source SAC tenant
  2. A target SAC tenant configured for Seamless Planning

Step-by-Step Migration Process

Below are the high-level steps required to migrate a model to Seamless Planning.

Step 1 – Build a Content Package on the Source Tenant

Navigate to the Content Network and create an export package containing:

  • The planning model
  • Associated public dimensions
  • Dependent objects

Figure 1: Build your Content Network package

Step 2 – Share the Content Package

Share the package with the SAC tenant configured for Seamless Planning.

Step 3 – Prepare the Destination Tenant

On the Seamless Planning tenant:

  • Ensure SAP Datasphere is configured as the persistence layer
  • Delete any models or public dimensions included in the package

Step 4 – Import the Package

Begin the import process on the Seamless Planning tenant.

During import, you will be prompted to select the Default Data Storage Location.

This is where the persistence flag is changed.

Figure 2: Select the SAP Datasphere Space to persist the model

Important: The storage location cannot be changed after import. If you select the wrong workspace, the model must be deleted and re-imported.

Step 5 – (Optional) Expose the Fact Table in Datasphere

After import, you may choose to expose the model’s fact table in SAP Datasphere for downstream consumption. This step is optional but recommended if plan data will be consumed in Datasphere views, transformation flows, or intelligent apps.

In the Model Designer:

  1. Navigate to Data Storage
  2. Click Edit
    Figure 3: Click the Edit button

     

  3. Select “Expose Data Foundation as Fact Table in SAP Datasphere” and provide Business and Technical Names
    Figure 4: Edit the Fact Table details
  4. After Clicking on the OK button you should see the status has been changed to EXPOSEDFigure 5: The Status is now EXPOSED

     

3️⃣Handling Large Data Volumes

The most common migration challenge when moving to Seamless Planning is related to data volume.

When exporting a model through the Content Network, the export package may fail if the included dataset exceeds supported limits. SAP does not publicly document an exact threshold, but customers frequently encounter failures when the package size approaches approximately 2GB or when the dataset exceeds roughly 1–2 million records.

If this occurs, the model must be exported without data, and the data must be migrated separately.

Understanding the Constraints

There are two important technical limits to consider:

  • Content Network export size limit (~2GB package size)
  • OData Import job limit (~10 million records per job)

These limits directly impact your migration strategy.

Option 1 – Standard OData Import (Up to ~10M Records per Job)

If your model contains fewer than 10 million records — or can easily be divided into manageable slices — SAC’s standard OData import functionality is sufficient.

How It Works

  1. Create an OData connection on the target (Seamless Planning) tenant
  2. Point it to the source model endpoint:
    <sac_url>/api/v1/dataexport/providers/sac/<model_id>
  3. Create import jobs with appropriate filters

Figure 6: Create a new OData Services ConnectionFigure 7: Create a new Import Job and connect to the FactData tableFigure 8: Edit Filter to ensure < 10M records are returned

When This Works Well

  • Models under ~10M records
  • Clear filtering dimensions (Version, Date, etc.)
  • Plan data only (Budget / Forecast / Plan)

The Challenge

Each OData import job is limited to approximately 10 million records.

For models containing significantly larger datasets (for example, 100 million records or more), manually creating multiple filtered import jobs can quickly become time-consuming and error-prone.

Administrators may need to partition the data by Version, Time, Region, Business Unit, or other dimensions in order to keep each job within the supported limits.

This is where automation becomes particularly valuable.

Option 2 – Separate Plan and Actual Data

In many customer scenarios, Actual data does not need to be copied.

Instead:

  • Reconnect Actual data to the system of record (e.g., S/4HANA)
  • Use a Live Version for Actual
  • Only migrate Plan / Budget / Forecast versions

This approach:

  • Reduces migration volume
  • Simplifies slicing
  • Aligns with best practice architecture

If your Plan/Budget/Forecast data remains under 10M records, you can import it using the standard OData approach described above.

More information about using Live Versions within the context of Seamless Planning can be found in the following blog post:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/unlocking-the-next-chapter-of-seamless-planning-in-sap-business-data-cloud/ba-p/14243864 

Option 3 – Automated API-Based Migration (Recommended for Large Models)

If:

  • Your model exceeds 10M records
  • Filtering OData jobs becomes untenable

Then a programmatic approach is recommended.

To address this challenge, I have created a Google Colab notebook that automates the migration process.

  • Exports data from the source model using the SAC Data Export API.
  • Imports the data into the target model using the SAC Data Import API.
  • Automatically batches requests to handle large data volumes.
  • Supports parallel processing to significantly reduce execution time.
  • Requires minimal technical setup and can be executed directly in a browser.

Why Google Colab?

Google Colab is:

  • Free
  • Browser-based
  • No local installation required
  • Hosted Python environment

It allows customers to run controlled API-based migration without setting up infrastructure.

How to Use the Colab Notebook

Step 1 – Open the Notebook

https://colab.research.google.com/drive/15jX6Atds-25kGHXHsSpUHX2tOhe6XBbj?usp=sharing 

When opened, you will be presented with the following parameter form:

Figure 9: Google Colab form

Step 2 – Save a Copy to Your Drive

Under the File menu select “Save a copy in Drive.”

⚠ If you do not save a copy, your parameters will not persist between sessions.

Figure 10: Save a Copy in Drive

Step 3 – Create OAuth Clients on Both SAC Tenants

The notebook requires OAuth authentication to both:

  • Source tenant
  • Target tenant

To create an OAuth client, within your SAP Analytics Cloud tenant:

  1. Navigate to Administration
  2. Select App Integration
  3. Choose + Add an OAuth Client

You will need:

  • Client ID
  • Client Secret
  • Token URL

The video below walks you through the process of creating an OAuth Client:

Step 4 – Enter Parameters

Once you have created the OAuth Client on both SAP Analytics Cloud tenants you will can switch back to the Google Colab form and update the parameters:

  • Source tenant URL
  • Target tenant URL
  • Source model ID
  • Target model ID
  • OAuth credentials
  • Import Job Settings

Then click Run.

The video below walks you through the process of running the Google Colab Notebook:

The notebook will:

  • Automatically generate groupings based on selected dimensions
  • Run parallel export jobs
  • Upload data in batches
  • Display progress logs

When Should You Use This Method?

  • Data exceeds 10M records
  • Manual slicing would require excessive jobs
  • Accuracy and repeatability are critical
  • Migration must be executed multiple times

In customer engagements involving 100M+ records, this method significantly reduces effort and risk.

4️⃣Handling Group Membership

Another commonly overlooked migration issue involves Teams and user membership.

When content is exported through the Content Network, team structures are transported to the target tenant. However, user assignments within those teams are not included in the export.

As a result, teams will appear on the destination tenant but will initially contain no users.

This behavior is by design.

Why Does This Happen?

The Content Network does not export users.

  • Identity Provider (IdP) configuration
  • Tenant-specific user management

Step 1 – Ensure Users Exist on the Target Tenant

  • Configure the IdP on the new tenant
  • Import users into SAC
  • Validate user IDs match the source tenant

If user IDs differ, group membership restoration will fail.

Step 2 – Restore Group Membership

Option A – Google Colab Automation

  • Reads group membership from the source tenant
  • Recreates membership assignments on the target tenant
  • Automates bulk processing

Similar to the Data load section, I have shared a Google Colab Notebook that can be used to copy the Team membership from one SAC tenant to another SAC tenant. You can access the notebook at the following link:

https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1o87IqqvKY2mcbL0EcH9Wff_dw6VBA-GI?usp=sharing 

The Google Colab form will have similar form parameters as the Data Copy notebook:

Figure 11: Google Colab Notebook for populating SAC Team membership

Watch the video below for step-by-step instructions on how to configure and run the Google Colab notebook:

Option B – Custom Widget (“Update Teams”)

The next approach is very similar to the Google Colab approach, however the code is wrapped in a custom widget rather than provided as a Google Colab notebook. This approach provides a bit better user experience, however does require the added step of installing the custom widget.

The Custom Widget will provide a Ux that allows users to:

  • Compare source and target groups
  • Update membership interactively
  • Synchronize assignments

The custom widget itself can be found here:
https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/113tk8dAMygIxQyEIj2JKSDBmmsKwisDD?usp=drive_link 

Watch the following video for step-by-step instructions on how to configure and use the Custom Widget:

5️⃣Handling Comments

Currently, comments are not migrated through the Content Network.

Current Limitation

Although comments can technically be accessed through the Optimized Design Experience (ODE) API, exporting and reimporting comments using this method is extremely slow and is not practical for large production environments.

For this reason, the ODE API approach is generally not recommended for migration scenarios involving significant volumes of comments.

The Only Viable Method Today

At present, the recommended approach for migrating comments is to request a backend database copy through SAP Support.

  1. Your SAP contact (for example a CSM, CSP, or Support representative) must raise an FPA12 support ticket.
  2. The ticket should include both the source and destination SAC tenant details.
  3. The request should specify that the comment database must be copied between tenants.

During this process, SAP Cloud Operations will copy the entire comment database from the source tenant to the destination tenant.

This process overwrites the destination comment database completely.

⚠ Critical Warning

Any comments added to the destination tenant prior to the database copy will be permanently overwritten.

It is expected that this limitation will be addressed in 2026 but at the time of writing this blog this is the recommended approach.

6️⃣FAQ & Summary

Can I change the persistence layer of an existing model?

Currently, there is no migration wizard which allows converting an existing model to a Seamless Planning model.

Can I simply have Cloud Ops do a backend migration?

Having SAP perform the replication on the backend will not convert the models to Seamless Planning. The option to change the persistence layer is only available when importing via the Content Network. Replicating the system via the backend will actually create more work as you will need to delete all references to the models which you want to convert to Seamless Planning before performing the migration steps outlined in this blog.

Can I migrate within a single tenant?

In most scenarios, a single-tenant migration is not possible if:

  • The dataset exceeds the Content Network export limits.
  • Existing comments must be preserved during the migration.

Do I need to migrate every model?

Not necessarily, you may choose a hybrid approach such as:

  • Deploy high-priority models to Datasphere
  • Leave low-priority models on public SAC

This approach will use the Datasphere resources for your high-priority models and the SAC resources for the models that remain local to the SAC tenant.

Conclusion

Migrating to Seamless Planning represents both a persistence change and an architectural evolution in how planning models interact with enterprise data.

Although the migration process itself is relatively straightforward, several practical challenges must be addressed, including large data volumes, team membership restoration, and comment migration.

With appropriate preparation and the use of automation tools where necessary, each of these challenges can be addressed with a reliable and repeatable approach.

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud for the Business Analyst: turning raw data to trusted and actionable insight
entry.content:
If you’re a business analyst in a center of excellence or embedded in a line-of-business team, you know the moment. It’s late in the day, you’re juggling closing activities, and someone pings you: “Our KPI moved. What changed?” You open your dashboard, spot the variance, and start tracing the root cause, only to hit the usual constraints: the definition of the KPI is unclear, the numbers don’t match what Finance sees, and the clock is not on your side.

Many of us have been in that seat. And the hardest part isn’t building a nice visualization; it’s getting to a trusted and actionable insight fast enough that the business can do something with it. When definitions conflict across teams, when data is scattered across SAP and non-SAP systems, and when every new question turns into a new item in the request queue, time-to-decision becomes the issue.

That’s where SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) aims to help business analysts: it harmonizes SAP application data and external data while preserving business semantics, so analytics and AI can stay trustworthy and practical for day-to-day fact-based decisions.

Why “trusted” is what makes “fast” possible

We don’t lose time because we can’t analyze, we lose time because we can’t connect and align. One team’s “net revenue” becomes another team’s “net revenue after returns.” One pipeline forecast uses an updated hierarchy while another uses last week’s mapping. The outcome is predictable: rework, credibility damage, and the dreaded “Which number is right?” loop.

As a business analyst, you succeed when you turn business data into actionable insight, bridging business users and data experts through validation, visualization, and clear communication. The path to faster insight-to-action starts when the platform reduces ambiguity and makes consistent definitions easier to reuse.

BDC’s value for business analysts shows up through three practical use cases—agentic analytics, data storytelling, and composable analytics—all aimed at accelerating the route to trusted, actionable answers.

Agentic analytics: when the platform helps you spot the signal

Agentic analytics are AI-driven, context-aware analytics that continuously monitor changing data conditions and deliver relevant insights and recommendations with minimal human intervention. In real terms, it’s the difference between discovering the KPI variance in tomorrow’s meeting versus being alerted early enough to act today.

BDC emphasizes two enablers here: AI-powered intelligent applications that surface patterns and anomalies, and a context-rich data foundation that unifies SAP and third-party data into a harmonized semantic layer, so the insight is explainable and based on consistent meaning.

When you’re under pressure, closing, forecast updates, pipeline reviews, this matters because it reduces the time you spend hunting for “what changed” and increases the time you can spend on “what should we do next.”

Data storytelling: turn analysis into a decision, not a debate

Even when you have the right answer, stakeholders still need the “why” in plain language. Data storytelling augments dashboards and visualizations with narrative context so decision-makers understand implications and act.

BDC highlights automated narratives (natural language generation that turns charts into written explanations) and in-context collaboration (comments, annotations, and sharing directly on dashboards). This directly supports the reality of LoB-embedded analysis: you’re not just producing charts, you’re aligning people around what the KPI means, why it moved, and what action to take.

Composable analytics: keep insight in the flow of work

Composable analytics treats models, metrics, and visualization widgets as reusable building blocks that can be combined and embedded into applications or workflows. For a business analyst, this is about scale: fewer one-off rebuilds, more reusable KPI logic, and more ways to deliver insight where decisions are made.

BDC positions open integration and embedding plus a unified semantic layer and reusable metrics as the foundation. The practical win is consistency: when you reuse a KPI definition across dashboards (and across teams), the conversation shifts from “whose number is right?” to “what are we going to do about it?”

A practical, analyst-first workflow to cut time-to-insight

Here’s the workflow you wish you’d had earlier, optimized for faster time to trusted and actionable insight:

  1. Start with governed building blocks: anchor your dashboard and KPI logic on curated models or data products so definitions don’t drift week to week.
  2. Validate fast when pressure hits: when a variance appears during closing or forecast cycles, run targeted ad-hoc checks without abandoning governed definitions.
  3. Explain the “why,” not just the “what”: pair visuals with narrative so stakeholders understand the root cause and the next action.
  4. Reuse and embed what works: treat your KPIs, metrics, and story components as reusable assets so every pipeline review doesn’t require a rebuild.

That’s the shift from “I made a report” to “I accelerated a decision.”

Make your next business question easier than the last

If you’re a business analyst, you’re going to keep getting the same class of questions: KPI movement, pipeline change, forecast risk, closing variance, root cause. The difference is whether answering them feels like a scramble, or a repeatable workflow grounded in trusted definitions and faster insight loops.

To go further, browse the Business Analyst resources in this interest group and build familiarity with how SAP Business Data Cloud supports agentic analytics, data storytelling, and composable analytics in real analyst workflows.

If you want, share in the comments: What’s the business question you get most often during closing, forecasting, or pipeline reviews—and what usually slows you down: time, definitions, or conflicting numbers?

Resources

Blog Post: What is a Business Data Fabric?

Browse the latest analytics resources on SAP.com

entry.title: Run Datasphere Task Chains from SAC Multi-Actions via External API Step
entry.content:
Abstract

With the availability of the Task Chain public API, you can now start SAP Datasphere task chains directly from SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Multi-Actions using the External API step. This enables more automated end-to-end processes that require coordinated actions in both SAC and Datasphere (DSP).

In this article, we cover the prerequisites and detailed setup for starting DSP task chains from SAC Multi-Actions using the External API step. To connect the dots, we also include a simplified seamless planning scenario that demonstrates the integration end to end.

Prerequisites

  • SAP Datasphere provides a public API for Task Chains starting in DSP 2026.02. Reference: https://api.sap.com/api/DatasphereTasks/overview

  • A technical user must already be enabled via the OAuth Client definition in Datasphere. This allows you to create a technical user with specific authorizations to call the public APIs and provides more flexible and controlled security management.

  • The SAC Multi-Actions External API step supports calling the Task Chain API starting in SAC 2026.02 (2026.QRC1).

Configuration

SAP Datasphere (DSP)

1) Prepare the Task Chain

Create a task chain in Datasphere:

task chain defined in DSP

2) Create an OAuth Client

Create an OAuth Client in Datasphere with the following settings:

  • Purpose: Select Technical User

  • Roles: Assign a role that has access to the space where the task chain is located

  • Authorization: Select Client Credentials

oauth client definition in DSP

3) Review the API Definition

Refer to the Task Chain API documentation here:

Overview | Tasks | SAP Business Accelerator Hub

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC)

1) Create a Connection to Datasphere

Create an http api connection to Datasphere which will be leveraged by multi-actions API step:

Create http api connection in SAC

2) Configure a Multi-Action with an External API Step

Because the Task Chain API runs asynchronously, you typically configure:

  • One API call to start the task chain

  • A second API call to poll and retrieve the execution status

Step A: Configuration of Starting the Task Chain

In the Generic API Settings section:

Request fields

  • Method: POST

  • API URL: https://<host name>/api/v1/datasphere/tasks/chains/<dsp space name>/run/<technical name of task chain>

Generic API settings

Response fields

  • Select Get API trigger status from HTTP status code and response body.

response setting

Click Configure Fields Mapping and define the expected response format, for example:

{
  "logId": 123456
}

Map the logId field in response to Job ID:

response fields mapping

Leave the status mapping empty. The API step will use the HTTP status code to determine whether the task chain was started successfully.

Step B: Configuration of Polling the Execution Status

In the Get API Execution Result section:

  • Method: Select Asynchronous Return

  • Polling API URL: https://<host name>/api/v1/datasphere/tasks/logs/<dsp space id>/${jobId}

In Response fields, select Get API execution result from HTTP status code and response body:

query api setting

Click Configure Fields Mapping and define the expected response format, for example:

{
 "status": ""
}

In Fields Mapping, map the status field to Status:

query response fields mapping

In Status Mapping, define the following mappings:

API Step Status ValueExternal Application Status Value
DONECOMPLETED
FAILEDFAILED
IN_PROCESSRUNNING

Run the Multi-Action

Run the Multi-Action. The API step completes when the Task Chain execution finishes:

Task chain monitor in DSP

multi-actions notification

Sample scenario

Below is a mimic example of using a Datasphere task chain in SAC Multi-Actions for a seamless planning workflow:

Planners enter Gross Revenue by Customer in SAC; a Datasphere Task Chain then runs a Transformation Flow that enriches the plan with customer attributes and computes Net Revenue into a curated fact table, which is exposed via an Analytic Model for live analysis in the SAC story.

  • Plan in SAC: Planners plan Gross Revenue by Date, Customer, Version in SAC.

  • Enrich in Datasphere: Customer attributes such as Segment (e.g., Enterprise/SMB), Industry, and Discount Rate are maintained in Datasphere.

  • Compute in Datasphere: Net Revenue = Gross × (1 − DiscountRate)
